title: COBOL: INITIALIZE on a group item does not reset its child fields
description:
INITIALIZE applied to a group item name may not iterate over the group's flattened child fields. The layout is a flat FieldLayout map; group items appear as entries but the INITIALIZE lowering code iterates over stmt.operands and resolves each name directly. If the name maps to the group-level entry rather than to each child, child fields are not reset.

Root cause: lower_arithmetic.py (INITIALIZE block, ~line 271-281) calls emit_field_encode for each operand. A group item's FieldLayout entry has a computed byte_length covering all children, but INITIALIZE should recursively reset each leaf field to its type-appropriate default (spaces for alphanumeric, zeros for numeric), not write a blanket byte range.

Example:
  01 WS-REC.
    05 WS-A PIC X(3).
    05 WS-B PIC 9(4).
  INITIALIZE WS-REC.
WS-A should be spaces, WS-B should be zeros. Writing the group entry as a single region with a mixed default is incorrect.

Affected file: interpreter/cobol/lower_arithmetic.py (~line 264-281).
acceptance_criteria:
1. INITIALIZE of a group item sets each alphanumeric child to spaces and each numeric child to zeros.
2. Nested group items are recursively reset (grandchildren, etc.).
3. Integration test: group with mixed PIC X and PIC 9 children; INITIALIZE group; assert each child has type-correct default.
4. INITIALIZE of a leaf field continues to work correctly.
close_reason: Closed

title: COBOL: INITIALIZE on a redefining field clobbers the redefined field's bytes
description:
INITIALIZE applied to a REDEFINES field correctly identifies the field's offset in the layout, but because REDEFINES fields share the same byte offset as the field they redefine, INITIALIZE overwrites the shared byte range with spaces or zeros, destroying whatever the redefined field held.

Root cause: lower_arithmetic.py (INITIALIZE lowering, ~line 264-281) looks up the FieldLayout for the operand by name and emits a WriteRegion at that field's offset and byte_length. Since REDEFINES fields share an offset (data_layout.py:75-79), this has the side-effect of also initializing the redefined field's bytes.

Example:
  01 WS-NUM PIC 9(5) VALUE 12345.
  01 WS-CHARS REDEFINES WS-NUM PIC X(5).
  INITIALIZE WS-CHARS.
After INITIALIZE WS-CHARS, WS-NUM should still read as 12345, but its bytes have been space-filled.

Related: data_layout.py:228-268 (_fix_redefines_offsets), lower_arithmetic.py INITIALIZE block.
acceptance_criteria:
1. INITIALIZE of a redefining field resets only that field's view (space-fills alphanumeric, zero-fills numeric).
2. After INITIALIZE WS-CHARS (alphanumeric overlay), reading WS-NUM (original numeric) still returns its pre-INITIALIZE value — no byte corruption.
3. Integration test: write a known value to original field, INITIALIZE the redefining field, assert original is unchanged.
4. INITIALIZE of the original field correctly resets the shared bytes.
close_reason: Closed

title: COBOL: all arithmetic uses Python float — silent precision loss for fixed-point fields
description:
Every COBOL arithmetic operation (ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, DIVIDE, COMPUTE) routes through a Python float (IEEE 754 double, ~15-17 significant digits) as the intermediate value. Fields with PIC clauses requiring more than 15 combined integer+decimal digits silently lose precision. No error is raised; the truncated value is re-encoded back into the field.

Root cause: lower_arithmetic.py:103-110 constructs a Binop IR instruction that operates on the decoded float values. emit_context.py:394-444 (emit_decode_field) decodes all numeric categories to float before arithmetic. The encoding back in emit_encode_numeric (emit_context.py:341-392) operates on the post-rounded float result.

Example: PIC S9(15)V9(10) contains 25 significant digits. After one ADD operation, the result is rounded to 15-17 digits, corrupting the last 8-10 decimal places. The COBOL standard requires exact fixed-point arithmetic.

Affected files:
- interpreter/cobol/lower_arithmetic.py:103-110
- interpreter/cobol/emit_context.py:341-444
- interpreter/cobol/binary.py:78-105 (decode returns float)
- interpreter/cobol/comp3.py:83-125 (decode returns float)
- interpreter/cobol/zoned_decimal.py:73-105 (decode returns float)
acceptance_criteria:
1. COMPUTE x = 0.1 + 0.2 where x is PIC 9V99 stores exactly 0.30 (not 0.30000000000000004).
2. A field PIC 9(10)V9(10) survives 100 ADD 0.0000000001 TO field operations and holds the correct exact value.
3. All existing COBOL arithmetic integration tests continue to pass.
4. No decode function returns Python float for fixed-point categories; all return exact integer-scaled values.

title: CI: import-linter fails — stale module paths in .importlinter
description: .importlinter references interpreter.executor and interpreter.backend which were reorganized to interpreter.vm.executor and interpreter.llm.backend. lint-imports fails in CI with 'Module interpreter.executor does not exist.'
close_reason: Updated interpreter.executor → interpreter.vm + interpreter.handlers, interpreter.backend → interpreter.llm. Added ignore for pre-existing symbol_table import. Both contracts pass.

title: VM scope chain: STORE in called function doesn't propagate writes to caller/enclosing scope
description: STORE inside a called function always writes to the current StackFrame local_vars. When the frame is popped on return, the write is lost. This affects ALL languages: Python global, JS closure writes, Java/Kotlin/Scala static field mutation from methods, Go package-level vars, C/C++ globals. Root cause: _handle_store only writes to vm.current_frame without checking parent frames. Workaround: self.field / this.field uses STORE_FIELD which writes to the heap (works correctly). This is why Rosetta bubble_sort excludes Scala — the method can't modify the object's arr field.
design: Approach: Add DECL_VAR opcode to IR. DECL_VAR always creates in current frame. STORE_VAR walks scope chain, updates first match, creates local if not found. Frontends emit DECL_VAR for declarations (let/var/val/int x = ...) and STORE_VAR for assignments (x = ...). Tree-sitter already distinguishes these as variable_declaration vs assignment_expression. VM change is small: _handle_store_var walks reversed call_stack checking local_vars for existing variable.

title: P0: test_function_type_subtype_of_any asserts the opposite of its name
description: In test_type_graph.py, test_function_type_subtype_of_any asserts 'not is_subtype_expr()' but name/docstring say 'subtype of Any'. Name and docstring are inverted — should be test_function_type_not_subtype_of_any.

title: COBOL: GOBACK / EXIT PROGRAM lowering — verify emits Return_ not Halt
description:
STOP RUN was fixed to emit Return_ instead of Halt (so subprogram execution returns to the caller rather than terminating the VM). GOBACK and EXIT PROGRAM are two other COBOL subprogram exit verbs that should have the same semantics:

- GOBACK: returns control to the caller. In the main program it behaves like STOP RUN (terminates). In a subprogram it returns to the calling program.
- EXIT PROGRAM: same as GOBACK in a subprogram; no-op in the main program.

Need to verify (and fix if necessary) that lower_procedure_division.py or the relevant lowering site emits Return_() for both verbs in subprogram context.

**Files to check:**
- interpreter/cobol/lower_procedure_division.py — look for GOBACK / EXIT PROGRAM statement handling
- interpreter/cobol/cobol_statements.py — GobackStatement, ExitProgramStatement definitions
- interpreter/cobol/lower_stop.py or wherever STOP RUN is handled (for reference impl)

If either verb emits Halt, change to Return_. If neither is implemented, add lowering.

**Distinction from STOP RUN:** STOP RUN in the main program should still halt (or emit Halt). GOBACK/EXIT PROGRAM should always emit Return_.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A COBOL subprogram ending with GOBACK returns to caller; integration test verifies caller continues executing after the CALL.
2. A COBOL subprogram ending with EXIT PROGRAM returns to caller.
3. GOBACK in the main program terminates (emits Halt or equivalent).
4. Unit tests for each verb's lowering.
5. @covers(CobolFeature.GOBACK) and @covers(CobolFeature.EXIT_PROGRAM).

title: COBOL: CALL USING BY REFERENCE — params region construction in lower_call.py
description:
Currently lower_call.py (interpreter/cobol/lower_call.py:27-58) ignores stmt.using entirely and passes the caller's whole WS region as both params_reg and results_reg to CallWithMemory. This is incorrect for CALL USING BY REFERENCE.

The fix is entirely in lower_call.py:

**Caller side (lower_call.py):**
1. Compute total params region size = sum of FieldLayout.byte_length for each CallUsingParam in stmt.using (resolved via ctx.resolve_field_ref(param.name, materialised)).
2. Emit AllocRegion(size=total_bytes) → %params_reg.
3. For each (i, param) in enumerate(stmt.using): resolve field from WS, emit LoadRegion(ws_reg, fl.offset, fl.byte_length) → %tmp, emit WriteRegion(%params_reg, cumulative_offset, %tmp, fl.byte_length). Cumulative offset = sum of byte_lengths of prior params.
4. Emit CallWithMemory(func_name=stmt.program, params_reg=%params_reg, results_reg=%params_reg).
5. For each BY REFERENCE param (param_type == 'REFERENCE'): emit LoadRegion(%params_reg, cumulative_offset, fl.byte_length) → %tmp, emit WriteRegion(ws_reg, fl.offset, %tmp, fl.byte_length).

**Callee side:** No change needed. lower_sectioned_data_division already emits LoadVar('__params_region') → lk_reg. The callee reads LINKAGE fields at their natural byte offsets (which start at 0 and are laid out consecutively). As long as caller USING field sizes match callee LINKAGE field sizes by position (standard COBOL practice), the byte layout aligns.

_handle_call_with_memory (interpreter/handlers/calls.py) already injects params_reg as __params_region into the callee frame — no change there.

**Key types:**
- CallUsingParam.name: str, CallUsingParam.param_type: str ('REFERENCE', 'CONTENT', 'VALUE') — interpreter/cobol/cobol_statements.py:671
- AllocRegion, LoadRegion, WriteRegion — interpreter/instructions.py:876-940
- EmitContext.resolve_field_ref(name, materialised) → (ResolvedFieldRef, region_reg) — interpreter/cobol/emit_context.py:182
acceptance_criteria:
1. CALL 'SUBPROG' USING BY REFERENCE WS-A passes WS-A bytes to callee's LINKAGE field at offset 0.
2. Callee modifies LINKAGE field 0; caller's WS-A is updated after return.
3. Unit test: lower_call emits AllocRegion + WriteRegion before CallWithMemory and LoadRegion + WriteRegion after.
4. Integration test: two-module COBOL program; SUBPROG doubles a value passed BY REFERENCE; caller verifies updated value in WS.
5. @covers(CobolFeature.CALL_USING) and @covers(CobolFeature.SECTION_LINKAGE).
6. feature_coverage_audit.py shows both features covered.

title: COBOL: CANCEL verb resets subprogram singleton WS to initial values
description: COBOL's CANCEL statement resets a subprogram to its initial state: WORKING-STORAGE returns to VALUE-clause values and LOCAL-STORAGE is re-initialised on the next call. In the singleton model (red-dragon-8rbw), each subprogram owns a HeapObject with a ws_handle field pointing to its persistent WS region. CANCEL must: (1) re-allocate a fresh WS region for the named program (with VALUE-clause initialisations applied), (2) store the new region address into the singleton's ws_handle field, overwriting the old handle. Implementation touches: interpreter/cobol/lower_call.py (emit CancelStatement lowering), interpreter/handlers/calls.py (new CANCEL handler), interpreter/cobol/cobol_statements.py.
acceptance_criteria:
1. CANCEL 'SUBPROG' resets SUBPROG's WS region to VALUE-clause initial values.
2. Next CALL to SUBPROG after CANCEL sees fresh WS (not values from prior calls).
3. Integration test: call SUBPROG (mutate WS), CANCEL SUBPROG, call again, assert WS is back to initial VALUE.
4. Programs not named in CANCEL are unaffected.
5. CANCEL of an unknown program name is a no-op (matches GnuCOBOL behaviour).

title: COMPUTE source ref_mod: WS-FIELD(start:length) in arithmetic expressions not supported
description: COBOL allows reference modification in COMPUTE expressions: COMPUTE WS-RESULT = WS-FIELD(1:3) + 5. The COMPUTE lowering path (lower_compute.py) is separate from ADD/SUBTRACT/MULTIPLY/DIVIDE (lower_arithmetic.py) which was fixed in red-dragon-4q25.40. Currently extractValueStmtText in the Java bridge returns raw text like 'WS-FIELD(1:3)' for COMPUTE operands, and the Python lowering treats it as a plain string. Fix requires: (1) Java bridge: serializeCompute to detect Call nodes in the expression and emit ref_mod metadata; (2) Python AST: update ComputeStatement operand model; (3) lower_compute.py: apply STRING_SLICE + float() conversion when ref_mod present. Files: proleap-bridge/src/main/java/org/reddragon/bridge/StatementSerializer.java (serializeCompute), interpreter/cobol/cobol_statements.py (ComputeStatement), interpreter/cobol/lower_compute.py.
close_reason: Implemented: structured JSON expression tree from Java bridge, RefModNode/expr_from_dict in Python, ExprNode migration for ComputeStatement. Integration tests in TestComputeRefMod all passing.

title: MOVE target ref_mod: MOVE x TO WS-FIELD(start:length) partial write not supported
description: COBOL allows writing into a substring of a field: MOVE x TO WS-FIELD(2:3). Currently the bridge serializes MOVE targets as plain strings (no ref_mod metadata), and lower_move.py does a full-field encode-and-write. A partial write must: (1) Java bridge: detect ref_mod on the receiving field in serializeMove and emit a structured target object with ref_mod_start/ref_mod_length; (2) Python AST: MoveStatement.targets: list[str] → list[RefModOperand]; (3) lower_move.py: when target has ref_mod, read the full field bytes, overwrite only the slice bytes, write back. Related to completed issues red-dragon-4q25.38 (INSPECT), red-dragon-4q25.39 (DISPLAY), red-dragon-4q25.40 (arithmetic). Files: proleap-bridge/src/main/java/org/reddragon/bridge/StatementSerializer.java (serializeMove), interpreter/cobol/cobol_statements.py (MoveStatement), interpreter/cobol/lower_move.py.
close_reason: Already implemented: MoveStatement.target is RefModOperand, lower_move handles STRING_SPLICE for target ref_mod at lower_arithmetic.py:282-330, Java bridge serializes receiving calls via serializeMoveOperand. Integration tests at test_cobol_programs.py:3538 and 3564 confirm working.

title: Test assertion mismatch: test_renders_edges_grouped_by_source in test_dataflow_graph_panel.py
description: Test name claims: renders edges grouped by source. Assertions actually check: any('x' in line and 'Return(func_f_0)' in line for line in lines) — only checks that a single line contains both the source and target node name. The test uses a graph with a single source-target pair, so 'grouped by source' behavior (multiple edges per source appearing together) is never actually exercised or verified. File: tests/unit/test_dataflow_graph_panel.py:99
close_reason: Strengthened test to use two sources (a→{ret_f,ret_g}, b→{ret_f}); now asserts len(a_indices)==2, len(b_indices)==1, and max(a_indices)<min(b_indices) — directly verifying grouping by source.

title: COBOL: VALUE clause — initial values not confirmed to be written at program startup
description:
The VALUE clause is parsed from the ProLeap ASG and stored on CobolField.value (asg_types.py:50), but there is no clearly visible code path that writes these initial values into the byte region during Working-Storage initialization. The FieldLayout dataclass has no initial_value field; lower_data_division.py emits an ALLOC_REGION for the total byte length but the per-field VALUE initialization path is opaque or missing.

If VALUE clauses are silently ignored at runtime, programs that depend on initial values (e.g. counters initialized to zero, flags to 'N', sentinel strings) will produce incorrect results with no error.

The verified feature CLAUSE_VALUE exists (verified_features.py) and a test test_value_clause exists, but the test may only cover IR generation (unit test), not runtime execution of the initialized value.

Affected files:
- interpreter/cobol/asg_types.py:50
- interpreter/cobol/data_layout.py (FieldLayout has no initial_value)
- interpreter/cobol/lower_data_division.py:15-34
acceptance_criteria:
1. An integration test writes a program that reads WS-COUNTER (PIC 9(3) VALUE 42) without MOVEing anything to it; the displayed value is 42.
2. A PIC X(5) VALUE 'HELLO' field reads as 'HELLO' at program start without explicit MOVE.
3. A PIC 9(3) field with no VALUE clause reads as 000 at program start (default zero-fill).
4. VALUE clause on a REDEFINES field: the redefined bytes are initialized correctly per the redefining field's VALUE.
5. VALUE clause on a group item: all elementary children receive their individual VALUE initializations.
close_reason: Closed

title: COBOL: BLANK WHEN ZERO clause parsed but not enforced in numeric encoding
description:
The BLANK WHEN ZERO clause is parsed from the ProLeap ASG and stored in CobolTypeDescriptor.blank_when_zero (cobol_types.py:27, asg_types.py:71), but it is never consulted during encoding. A numeric field declared BLANK WHEN ZERO should display as spaces when its value is zero; instead it encodes normally as '00000' or equivalent.

Root cause: No code path in emit_context.py or ir_encoders.py checks the blank_when_zero flag before encoding. The flag exists in the type descriptor solely as stored data.

Example:
  01 WS-AMT PIC 9(5) BLANK WHEN ZERO.
  MOVE 0 TO WS-AMT.
  DISPLAY WS-AMT.
Should display '     ' (5 spaces). Currently displays '00000'.

Affected files:
- interpreter/cobol/cobol_types.py:27
- interpreter/cobol/asg_types.py:71
- interpreter/cobol/emit_context.py:341-392 (emit_encode_numeric — no blank_when_zero check)
acceptance_criteria:
1. A PIC 9(5) BLANK WHEN ZERO field encoded with value 0 produces 5 space bytes.
2. The same field with any non-zero value encodes normally (digits, not spaces).
3. Reading back a blanked field: decoding the 5 space bytes returns 0.
4. Integration tests cover: zero value (blanked), non-zero value (normal), zero after arithmetic (blanked).
close_reason: Implementation already in place: emit_encode_value checks blank_when_zero for compile-time values (emit_context.py:292), emit_encode_from_string wraps runtime encoding via _builtin_cobol_blank_when_zero (byte_builtins.py:477). Integration test test_blank_when_zero_mixed passes.

title: COBOL: JUSTIFIED RIGHT clause parsed but not enforced in alphanumeric encoding
description:
The JUSTIFIED RIGHT clause is parsed from the ProLeap ASG and stored in CobolTypeDescriptor.justified_right (cobol_types.py:25), but it is never consulted during encoding or decoding. Alphanumeric fields are always left-justified (right-padded with spaces) regardless of whether JUSTIFIED RIGHT is specified.

Root cause: alphanumeric.py:35 hardcodes s.ljust(length, EBCDIC_SPACE), which always left-justifies. Neither emit_encode_numeric (emit_context.py:341-392) nor the alphanumeric IR builder checks the justified_right flag on the type descriptor.

Example:
  01 WS-NAME PIC X(10) JUSTIFIED RIGHT.
  MOVE 'ABC' TO WS-NAME.
Should store '       ABC' (7 leading spaces). Currently stores 'ABC       ' (7 trailing spaces).

Affected files:
- interpreter/cobol/alphanumeric.py:35
- interpreter/cobol/emit_context.py:311-328 (alphanumeric encode path)
- interpreter/cobol/cobol_types.py:25 (flag stored but unused)
acceptance_criteria:
1. A PIC X(10) JUSTIFIED RIGHT field MOVEd 'ABC' stores '       ABC' (right-aligned).
2. A plain PIC X(10) field MOVEd 'ABC' still stores 'ABC       ' (left-aligned, unchanged behavior).
3. JUSTIFIED RIGHT with a value longer than the field truncates from the left (standard COBOL behavior).
4. Integration tests cover: short string right-justified, exact-length string, overlong string.
close_reason: Closed

title: COBOL: MOVE CORRESPONDING not implemented
description:
MOVE CORRESPONDING source TO target is not implemented. There are zero references to CORRESPONDING in the lowering codebase. MOVE CORRESPONDING is common in legacy COBOL programs that use it to copy fields between two group items by matching field names.

Required behavior: For each elementary item in the destination group that has a matching name in the source group (after stripping qualifications), perform a MOVE. Non-matching fields in source or destination are ignored. CORRESPONDING is recursive for nested groups.

Affected files (need new code):
- interpreter/cobol/cobol_statements.py (new MoveCorrespondingStatement)
- interpreter/cobol/lower_arithmetic.py or new lower_move_corresponding.py
- interpreter/cobol/data_layout.py (field name lookup for CORRESPONDING logic)
- ProLeap bridge / asg_types.py (verify CORRESPONDING is emitted in ASG)
acceptance_criteria:
1. MOVE CORRESPONDING src TO dst copies all fields whose names match between the two groups.
2. Fields in src with no counterpart in dst are silently ignored.
3. Fields in dst with no counterpart in src retain their existing values.
4. Nested groups: MOVE CORRESPONDING applies recursively to sub-groups with matching names.
5. Integration tests: two groups with 3 shared + 2 unique fields; assert shared fields copied, unique fields unchanged.
6. MOVE (non-CORRESPONDING) is unaffected.
close_reason: Implemented and tested: 4 integration tests passing (basic, partial overlap, no matches, multiple targets).

title: COBOL: ON SIZE ERROR / overflow detection not implemented in arithmetic
description:
The ON SIZE ERROR clause and arithmetic overflow detection are entirely absent from RedDragon's COBOL arithmetic lowering. When a computed result exceeds the capacity of the target field (e.g. 999 + 1 into PIC 9(3)), no error is raised, no ON SIZE ERROR branch is taken, and the result silently wraps or truncates.

Root cause: (1) ArithmeticStatement in cobol_statements.py:105-132 has no on_size_error field. (2) lower_arithmetic.py:103-110 emits a Binop with no post-computation bounds check. (3) emit_context.py:277-288 (encode dispatch) does not validate that the encoded value fits in the target byte_length.

Division by zero is a special case of the same problem: lower_arithmetic.py:105-110 emits a Binop(DIVIDE) with no guard, so division by zero either raises a Python ZeroDivisionError (unhandled) or produces undefined behavior.

Affected files:
- interpreter/cobol/cobol_statements.py:105-132
- interpreter/cobol/lower_arithmetic.py:103-116
- interpreter/cobol/emit_context.py:277-288
acceptance_criteria:
1. A program with ADD 999 TO PIC 9(3) ON SIZE ERROR DISPLAY 'overflow' executes the ON SIZE ERROR branch.
2. A program with DIVIDE 0 INTO X ON SIZE ERROR DISPLAY 'divzero' executes the ON SIZE ERROR branch.
3. Without ON SIZE ERROR, overflow does not produce a Python exception; behavior is undefined but consistent.
4. NOT ON SIZE ERROR branch is executed when no overflow occurs.
5. Integration tests cover: ADD overflow, SUBTRACT underflow, MULTIPLY overflow, DIVIDE by zero, each with and without ON SIZE ERROR.
close_reason: Implemented: emit_overflow_check in lower_arithmetic, div-by-zero guard, ON SIZE ERROR / NOT ON SIZE ERROR branching for ADD/SUBTRACT/MULTIPLY/DIVIDE. Integration tests in TestOnSizeError all passing.

title: COBOL: ROUNDED clause not implemented in arithmetic statements
description:
The ROUNDED clause on ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, DIVIDE, and COMPUTE is not implemented. When a result has more decimal digits than the target field, COBOL requires rounding the excess digits per the COBOL rounding rule (round half away from zero by default). Instead, the current implementation truncates the string representation, which silently produces the wrong value.

Root cause: lower_arithmetic.py:103-110 computes the Binop result and calls emit_to_string; no rounding step is applied before encoding to the target field. The statement dataclass (cobol_statements.py:105-132) likely has no 'rounded' flag on arithmetic statements.

Example:
  01 X PIC 9V9.
  COMPUTE X ROUNDED = 1.25.
Should store 1.3 (rounds up). Without ROUNDED, or with incorrect handling, stores 1.2 (truncates).

Affected files:
- interpreter/cobol/lower_arithmetic.py:103-110
- interpreter/cobol/cobol_statements.py (ArithmeticStatement)
acceptance_criteria:
1. ArithmeticStatement has a 'rounded' boolean field parsed from ProLeap ASG.
2. COMPUTE X ROUNDED = 1.25 where X is PIC 9V9 stores 1.3.
3. ADD 0.15 TO X ROUNDED where X is PIC 9V9 and X was 1.2 stores 1.4.
4. Without ROUNDED, result is truncated (not rounded) per existing behavior.
5. Integration tests cover: round up, round down, round half, negative values.

title: COBOL correctness gaps: memory layout, type handling, arithmetic & string ops (2026-04-16 audit)
description:
Comprehensive correctness gaps identified in 2026-04-16 audit of the COBOL memory layout, type handling, REDEFINES, encoding/decoding, and statement lowering subsystems. Three P0 bugs, seven P1 gaps, and nine P2 gaps. Pre-existing issues red-dragon-x3p7 (OCCURS DEPENDING ON) and red-dragon-cmu1 (SYNCHRONIZED) are related but filed separately.

Subsystems covered:
- interpreter/cobol/data_layout.py
- interpreter/cobol/emit_context.py
- interpreter/cobol/ir_encoders.py
- interpreter/cobol/lower_arithmetic.py
- interpreter/cobol/lower_string_inspect.py
- interpreter/cobol/cobol_types.py
- interpreter/cobol/alphanumeric.py
- interpreter/cobol/binary.py

title: Test assertion mismatch: test_block_comment_inside_binary_expression in test_java_frontend.py
description: Test name claims: block comment inside binary expression is handled correctly. Assertions actually check: only that len(ir) > 0 (IR is non-empty). Does not verify the comment is skipped or that the binary expression produces correct IR. File: tests/unit/test_java_frontend.py
close_reason: Fixed: assertions now verify == and || BINOPs are emitted and no comment-related SYMBOLICs appear

title: Test assertion mismatch: test_line_comment_inside_binary_expression in test_java_frontend.py
description: Test name claims: line comment inside binary expression is handled correctly. Assertions actually check: only that len(ir) > 0 (IR is non-empty). Does not verify the comment is skipped or that the binary expression produces correct IR. File: tests/unit/test_java_frontend.py
close_reason: Fixed: assertions now verify == and || BINOPs are emitted and no comment-related SYMBOLICs appear

title: Linker: demand-driven symbol resolution with scope isolation
description:
## Summary

Replace the current eager-merge linker with a demand-driven linker that follows import edges from the entry module and provides proper scope isolation between modules. This is the correct foundation for multi-file support and will fix the require() namespace problem as a natural consequence.

## Current Architecture (Problems)

The current linker in `interpreter/project/linker.py` uses **eager flatten**:

1. ALL modules' IR is concatenated in dependency order
2. ALL functions/classes from ALL modules are globally visible
3. No scope isolation — names leak across module boundaries
4. Name collisions between modules are undetected
5. `require()` returns a symbolic value because there's no module namespace object

This works by accident for small projects but is architecturally wrong.

## Proposed Architecture (Demand-Driven)

### Phase 1: Import graph analysis
- Start from entry module (e.g., `main.ts`)
- Walk import edges to discover which symbols are needed from which modules
- Build a **demand set**: `dict[Path, set[str]]` — which symbols are needed from each module

### Phase 2: Selective inclusion
- Only include IR for symbols in the demand set (functions, classes, top-level init code)
- Each module's top-level code is included only if the module is in the dependency graph
- Unused modules are excluded entirely

### Phase 3: Scope-aware name binding
- Each module gets its own scope in the merged IR
- Import statements create explicit bindings: `import { add } from './utils'` → bind local `add` to `utils::add`
- `import x = require('./y')` → construct namespace object with fields for each exported symbol
- Qualified references (`utils.add`) resolve through the namespace object

### Phase 4: (Optional) Tree-shaking
- Within included modules, only export symbols that are actually referenced
- Dead code elimination for unreferenced internal functions

## Key Design Decisions

1. **Namespace objects for require()**: When `import x = require('./module')`, the linker emits IR to construct an object with fields for each export. Pattern: `NEW_OBJECT → STORE_FIELD add → STORE_FIELD Greeter → STORE_VAR x`. This matches Node.js semantics.

2. **Direct binding for ESM**: `import { add } from './utils'` rewrites to direct `CALL_FUNCTION utils::add` — no namespace object needed. This already works in the current system (linker flattens and `add` is globally available), but should be made explicit rather than accidental.

3. **ExportTable drives inclusion**: `ModuleUnit.exports` already tracks functions, classes, and top-level variables. The demand-driven linker uses this to know what's available from each module.

## Files Affected

- `interpreter/project/linker.py` — Major rewrite: demand-driven resolution, scope-aware merging
- `interpreter/project/compiler.py` — Minor: pass demand set to linker
- `interpreter/project/types.py` — Possibly extend `LinkedProgram` with scope metadata
- `interpreter/project/resolver.py` — No change (already maps imports to paths)
- `interpreter/project/imports.py` — No change (already extracts import refs)

## Existing Related Issues

- **red-dragon-p5gh** (P1 bug, open): TS import_require_clause silently dropped — IR lowering is fixed, but runtime still produces symbolic values. This issue subsumes the remaining execution gap.
- **red-dragon-khfp** (P1 bug, closed): Linker VM dispatch mismatch — import stubs produce symbolic values
- **red-dragon-qyvn** (P1 bug, closed): Dependency modules' top-level code unreachable
- **red-dragon-lzae** (P1 bug, closed): Linker import stub dropper assumes CALL_FUNCTION immediately followed by DECL_VAR
- **red-dragon-co5y** (P2 task, closed): Original cross-module reference resolution

## Test Strategy

- Unit tests for demand set construction (given import graph, verify correct symbol sets)
- Unit tests for namespace object IR emission pattern
- Integration tests: `import { add } from './utils'; add(3, 4)` → `answer == 7`
- Integration tests: `import utils = require('./utils'); utils.add(3, 4)` → `answer == 7`
- Integration tests: name collision detection (two modules export same name)
- Regression: all existing multi-file tests must continue to pass

## Reference

Production linkers (webpack, rollup, esbuild, ld) all use demand-driven resolution:
- Start from entry points
- Follow import edges to discover reachable modules
- Each module keeps its own scope
- Only referenced exports are included (tree-shaking)
- Unreferenced code is dead-code-eliminated

title: Type inference coverage harness — measure UNKNOWN rates per language per algorithm
description: Add a new harness type that measures type inference completeness. For each (language, algorithm) pair from the Rosetta suite, compile the program, run type inference, and report: (a) total registers with types, (b) registers with UNKNOWN type, (c) total variables with types, (d) variables with UNKNOWN type, (e) coverage percentage. This directly implements Gap 11 from the strongly-typed IR analysis (docs/design/strongly-typed-ir.md). The harness should: 1. Reuse Rosetta programs as input corpus. 2. Compile and run type inference (no VM execution needed). 3. Assert minimum coverage thresholds per language tier: statically-typed languages (Java, Go, TypeScript, Rust, C, C++, Kotlin, Swift, Scala) should target higher coverage than dynamic languages (Python, JS, Ruby, PHP, Lua). 4. Output a summary table for human review. 5. Track coverage over time to detect regressions. File: tests/unit/test_type_inference_coverage.py. Related: red-dragon-ivy5 (strongly-typed IR).

title: ExecutionStats.llm_calls not incremented by LLMPlausibleResolver

The counter in run.py only tracks the LLM backend fallback path (llm.interpret_instruction), not the resolver path. Fix: add llm_call_count property to UnresolvedCallResolver base class, increment in LLMPlausibleResolver on each complete() call, wire into stats in run.py execute_cfg and execute_cfg_traced.
close_reason: Closed

title: LLM frontend: add DECL_VAR, CALL_CTOR, TRY_PUSH/TRY_POP opcodes
description:
## Problem

The LLM frontend (interpreter/llm/llm_frontend.py) only emits 19/33 IR opcodes. Four missing opcodes apply to mainstream languages and should be added:

### DECL_VAR
Currently the LLM uses STORE_VAR everywhere, losing the declare-vs-assign distinction. DECL_VAR is atomic declaration + initialization.

### CALL_CTOR
The prompt tells the LLM to use CALL_FUNCTION for constructors, losing type hints. CALL_CTOR carries a type_hint field needed by the type system.

### TRY_PUSH / TRY_POP
Exception handling (try/catch/finally) is common in Java/Python/JS. Without these, try/catch blocks produce no IR.

## Scope

1. Update the system prompt in LLMFrontendPrompts.SYSTEM_PROMPT to document the new opcodes
2. Update _parse_single_instruction() to handle the new opcode JSON shapes
3. Add integration tests that verify the LLM frontend emits these opcodes for appropriate source patterns
4. Skip COBOL-specific ops (region, continuation) and pointer ops — not relevant for LLM frontend targets

## Files
- `interpreter/llm/llm_frontend.py` — prompt + parser
- `tests/` — new integration tests
close_reason: Already implemented: opcodes documented in SYSTEM_PROMPT (llm_frontend.py lines 72-84), parser handles them via Opcode enum, unit tests exist in tests/unit/test_llm_frontend.py lines 147-426

title: Java frontend: lower constant_declaration (public static final)
description:
## Problem

The Java frontend emits `Symbolic(hint='unsupported:constant_declaration')` for `public static final` declarations instead of lowering the initialiser expression.

## Impact

Affects real-world [REDACTED] code — `GenericValue.java` lines 14-17 declare padding constants:

```java
public static final String PAD1 = "                ";
public static final String PAD2 = PAD1 + PAD1 + PAD1 + PAD1;
public static final String PAD3 = PAD2 + PAD2 + PAD2 + PAD2;
public static final String PAD  = PAD3 + PAD3 + PAD3 + PAD3;
```

These produce 4 symbolic values in Datp111a execution (the only remaining symbolics after Calendar/GregorianCalendar stdlib was added).

## Test

`tests/integration/test_java_constant_declaration.py::TestJavaConstantDeclaration::test_static_final_constants_are_concrete` (currently xfail)

## Fix

In the Java frontend, handle `constant_declaration` tree-sitter nodes by lowering the initialiser expression and emitting `DeclVar` with the result. The node structure is similar to `field_declaration` but includes modifiers `public static final`.
close_reason: Closed

title: Java frontend: qualified type names split into LOAD_VAR + LOAD_FIELD chain
description:
## Problem

Fully-qualified Java type references like `java.util.Arrays.fill(arr, val)` are lowered as a LOAD_VAR + LOAD_FIELD chain, producing cascading symbolic values (67 in [REDACTED] alone).

## Design: Namespace Tree + Runtime Library

### Architecture (3 layers)

**Layer 1 — Namespace Tree (compile-time data structure)**
- Tree of `NamespaceNode` mapping package paths to types
- Built from: (a) project classes via `compile_directory()`, (b) import declarations via `extract_imports()`, (c) optional JDK seed
- Each type node is a "join point" — everything before is namespace, everything after is member access
- `import java.util.Arrays` → registers `java → util → Arrays(type)` in the tree

**Layer 2 — Stub Types (type declarations in IR)**
- External types discovered via imports + AST usage scanning get real class declarations in the IR
- Methods/fields are declared based on what the source code actually uses
- Registered in the function table / registry so calls resolve normally
- Types without implementations → methods marked `external`, return symbolic

**Layer 3 — Runtime Library (concrete implementations)**
- Key standard library methods get real implementations (IR or builtin handlers)
- `Arrays.fill(arr, val)` — iterates and sets elements
- `Arrays.equals(a, b)` — element-wise comparison
- `Math.max(a, b)`, `Integer.parseInt(s)`, `Collections.emptyList()`, etc.
- Incrementally expanded based on what causes the most symbolics in real codebases
- Methods not implemented fall back to symbolic return (Layer 2)

### Resolution flow

```
Source:  java.util.Arrays.fill(arr, val)

1. Frontend sees field_access chain with undeclared root "java"
2. Walks namespace tree: java → util → Arrays (join point = type)
3. Emits: LOAD_VAR "java.util.Arrays" → CALL_METHOD "fill"
   (or direct CALL to resolved function if runtime library has it)
4. VM executes Arrays.fill concretely — no symbolics
```

### Namespace resolution algorithm (mirrors JLS §6.5)

```
Given chain: a.b.c.d(...)
1. Is "a" a declared local/param/field? → normal field access chain
2. Else → enter namespace resolution:
   Walk namespace tree: a → a.b → a.b.c
   Stop at first type node (join point)
   Emit qualified LOAD_VAR for the type
   Rest becomes normal member access
```

### Data structures

```python
@dataclass
class NamespaceNode:
    children: dict[str, NamespaceNode]   # sub-namespaces
    types: dict[str, QualifiedType]      # types at this level

@dataclass
class QualifiedType:
    qualified_name: str                  # "java.util.Arrays"
    methods: dict[str, MethodStub]       # known methods
    fields: dict[str, FieldStub]         # known static fields

@dataclass
class MethodStub:
    name: str
    arity: int                           # from usage
    implementation: Callable | None      # None = external/symbolic
```

### Sources for the namespace tree

1. **Project classes** — auto from compile_directory() registry
2. **Import declarations** — `import java.util.Arrays` → type at java.util.Arrays
3. **Wildcard imports** — `import java.util.*` → namespace at java.util, types resolved from usage
4. **AST usage scan** — `Arrays.fill(arr, val)` → Arrays has method fill(arity=2)
5. **JDK seed** (optional) — common java.*, javax.* types for cases with no explicit import

### No new opcodes needed

The namespace resolution happens entirely at compile time (frontend). The IR uses existing instructions (LOAD_VAR with qualified VarName, CALL_METHOD, etc.). No VM changes required except for runtime library builtin handlers.

## Impact

- Eliminates 67 symbolic values from [REDACTED] (LOAD_VAR chain cascade)
- With runtime library implementations, eliminates even more (concrete execution of std lib calls)
- Cross-language applicable: C# System.*, Python os.path.*, etc.

## Related

- Hex literal fix: commit 0a213b48
- Array .length fix: same commit
- C# .Length issue (red-dragon-y66p), Kotlin .size issue (red-dragon-9d98)
close_reason: Closed

title: VM returns symbolic for array .length field access
description:
## Problem

When the VM creates an array via NEW_ARRAY, subsequent `.length` field access returns a symbolic value instead of the concrete array size. This causes massive symbolic cascade in loops bounded by `arr.length`.

## Impact

In [REDACTED] ([REDACTED] project), [REDACTED].java's static initializer creates a MINUS[] array and loops `for(int i=0; i<0x7f; ++i)`. After fixing hex literal parsing (red-dragon-tmtx), the loop bound resolved but `arr.length` remained symbolic, generating 3080 symbolic values — still the dominant source (99%+ of all symbolics).

## Root Cause (suspected)

The VM's NEW_ARRAY handler likely creates a HeapObject but doesn't store the array length as a concrete field. When LOAD_FIELD with field name "length" executes, it finds no field and falls back to symbolic.

## Investigation Needed

1. Trace how NEW_ARRAY stores array metadata in the heap
2. Check how LOAD_FIELD handles "length" on array objects
3. Determine if array size is available at creation time
4. Fix: store length as a concrete field on array HeapObjects

## Files to investigate

- `interpreter/handlers/memory.py` — LOAD_FIELD handler
- `interpreter/handlers/collections.py` or similar — NEW_ARRAY handler
- `interpreter/vm/vm_types.py` — HeapObject structure
close_reason: Fixed: Java frontend emits StoreField with FieldKind.SPECIAL for array .length after NEW_ARRAY. VM LOAD_FIELD falls back to SPECIAL kind.

title: Java frontend emits hex/octal/binary literals as raw strings
description:
## Problem

The Java frontend's `lower_const_literal` emits hex (`0x7f`), octal (`0777`), and binary (`0b1010`) integer literals as raw string text. Python's `int()` coercion cannot parse these non-decimal formats, causing `eval_binop` to return UNCOMPUTABLE and generating symbolic values.

## Impact

In [REDACTED] ([REDACTED] project), `[REDACTED].java` has a static initializer `for(int i=0;i<0x7f;++i) MINUS[i]=0x70;` that generated 3428 symbolic values per run — 99.2% of all symbolics. Every iteration's comparison `i < "0x7f"` was UNCOMPUTABLE because the RHS was a string.

## Fix

Added `lower_java_integer_literal` in `interpreter/frontends/java/expressions.py` that parses hex/octal/binary to decimal strings before emitting CONST. Wired into Java frontend dispatch for all four integer literal node types.

## Files

- `interpreter/frontends/java/expressions.py` — `_parse_java_integer()` + `lower_java_integer_literal()`
- `interpreter/frontends/java/frontend.py` — dispatch table updated
- `tests/unit/test_java_frontend.py` — 5 unit tests (hex, hex+L, octal, binary, decimal unchanged)
- `tests/integration/test_java_frontend_execution.py` — 2 integration tests (hex comparison, hex loop bound)

## Remaining

After this fix, 3080 symbolic values still cascade from `arr.length` being symbolic (separate issue — VM doesn't concretely track array lengths).
close_reason: Fixed

See investigation_report_symbolic_values.md for full details.

title: Linker skips stdlib module initialization - System never initialized
description:
## Bug Description

When linking stdlib modules with user code, the module initialization sequence is broken. Specifically:

1. The merged IR contains System module setup code (instructions 191-198):
   - `call_ctor PrintStream` (line 195)
   - `new_object System` (line 196)
   - `store_field %83 out %82` (line 197) - sets System.out
   - `decl_var System %83` (line 198) - declares System variable

2. BUT the entry point CFG only chains through Math, String, PrintStream modules

3. System module entry (`java.lang.System.entry_System`) is NEVER visited during execution

4. Result: System.out is never initialized, all System.out.println() calls fall back to symbolic execution

5. Impact: [REDACTED] execution produces 192+ symbolic registers instead of concrete heap objects

## Evidence

From trace analysis:
```
Visited blocks: 19
  java.lang.Math.entry_Math ✓
  java.lang.String.entry_String ✓
  java.io.PrintStream.entry_PrintStream ✓
  (java.lang.System.entry_System - NOT VISITED) ✗
```

Entry block successors:
```
Entry block successors: [CodeLabel(value='java.lang.Math.entry_Math')]
```

Only Math is linked, missing: System, ArrayList, HashMap, List, Collection, Map

## Root Cause

The `_transform_module()` function in `interpreter/project/linker.py` creates module initialization code, but the CFG entry point doesn't properly chain through ALL modules in the topo_order. The entry block should sequentially visit each module's entry point.

## Files Involved

- `interpreter/project/linker.py` - `_transform_module()`, `link_modules()`
- `interpreter/cfg.py` - CFG construction may not connect module chains

## Related Issues

This explains why stdlib functions don't produce concrete output - the System singleton (with PrintStream out) is never actually created on the heap.

## Severity

HIGH - breaks stdlib functionality for any program using System.out
close_reason:
Fixed class initialization order bug. 

Changes made:
1. interpreter/frontends/_base.py: Moved emit_class_ref and DeclVar BEFORE branch instruction in _lower_class_def()
2. interpreter/frontends/java/declarations.py: Same fix for Java-specific lower_class_def()  
3. experiments/java_stdlib/stubs/java_lang_system.py: Restructured System module to put setup code before entry_System label

Results:
- Simple test: 2 concrete heap objects, 0 symbolic - WORKING
- Module initialization chain: All stdlib modules now properly connected - WORKING
- [REDACTED]: 192 symbolic registers remain, but this is a separate issue with load_var creating fresh symbolic values rather than reusing the concrete System singleton

TDD tests added: tests/unit/test_class_initialization_bug.py (2 tests, both passing)
All existing tests pass (93 Java frontend tests, 26 C# integration tests)

title: LinkedProgram execution needs entry_point support for two-phase dispatch
description: compile_project() returns a LinkedProgram with merged IR/CFG/registry, but there's no way to specify an entry_point function (e.g., main) for execution. The two-phase dispatch (preamble then function dispatch) currently lives in run(), which only accepts raw source strings. For multi-module projects, we need the caller to control which function to enter after the preamble completes. The preamble for a 41-file project is ~6000+ instructions of CONST+DECL_VAR pairs — without two-phase dispatch, execution exhausts max_steps on the preamble and never enters the target function. The fix should expose entry_point as a parameter somewhere in the LinkedProgram execution path, reusing the same two-phase logic from run().
close_reason: Implemented: EntryPoint type with function(predicate) and top_level() modes, run_linked() execution function, run() refactored to build LinkedProgram and delegate, compile_project() removed, compile_directory() simplified, 145 call sites migrated, ADR-130 written. 16 follow-up issues filed for per-frontend multi-module integration tests.

title: Topological sort rejects cyclic imports (legal in Java)
description: compile_project() calls topological_sort() which raises CyclicImportError for mutual imports like NaturalLibrary.java <-> AdabasCall.java. Java allows circular imports — the JVM handles them via lazy class loading. The topological sort in interpreter/project/resolver.py should tolerate cycles by placing cycle members in arbitrary order instead of raising. Found 2 cyclic edges among 41 files in a real multi-module Java project. The linker's namespace/rebase mechanism doesn't depend on strict topological order — it just needs all modules present.
close_reason: topological_sort now tolerates cycles by forcing remaining nodes into arbitrary order with a warning log

title: CALL_CTOR does not resolve to inner class constructors in single-file compilation
description: When using entry_point to jump directly into a function, CALL_CTOR produces a symbolic value instead of dispatching to the inner class constructor. The registry finds classes and functions, but the executor's CALL_CTOR handler doesn't match the constructor name to the class's __init__ label because the module-level preamble (which populates ClassRefs in scope) never runs. This causes all object construction to go symbolic. The gap is in the CALL_CTOR → class registry → __init__ label resolution chain. Fixed by two-phase execution: run module preamble first, then dispatch into target function.
close_reason: Two-phase execution implemented: module preamble runs before entry_point dispatch. All 4 constructor tests pass. 13,168 total tests pass.

title: Test assertion mismatch: test_repeat_until checks wrong opcode
description: File: tests/unit/test_lua_frontend.py. Claims repeat-until loop lowers correctly. Asserts Opcode.UNOP in opcodes, but repeat/until with condition 'x == 0' should produce BINOP (==), not UNOP. The assertion appears to check the wrong opcode.
close_reason: False positive — UNOP 'not' IS correct. The repeat-until frontend emits BINOP == then UNOP not to negate the until-condition for the loop-back branch. Verified by inspecting actual IR output.

title: Test assertion mismatch: test_raw_and_transitive_graphs
description: File: tests/unit/test_propagation.py. Claims raw graph has direct edges and transitive graph has indirect edges. Only asserts len(raw_graph) > 0 — no assertion on transitive_graph at all.
close_reason: Added transitive_graph assertion: len(transitive_graph) >= len(raw_graph).

title: Test assertion mismatch: test_return_last_expression_in_block

close_reason: Added assertion that RETURN operand references BINOP result register.

title: Test assertion mismatch: test_recursive_function_converges

Should emit CALL_FUNCTION require 'y' + STORE_VAR x — same pattern as JS CJS require. More common than import_alias — used when esModuleInterop isn't enabled. Light fix — detect import_require_clause child inside import_statement handler.

title: VM: nested function scoping leaks in Ruby/PHP/Lua

title: C#: byref param as method receiver — LOAD_FIELD on Pointer
description: When a ref parameter holds an object, calling a method on it via *ref dereference hits LOAD_FIELD '*' on the heap Pointer. xfail in test_csharp_frontend_execution.py::TestCSharpByrefEdgeCases::test_byref_param_as_method_receiver.
close_reason: Fixed: LOAD_INDIRECT on obj_*/arr_* with no field 0 now returns the Pointer itself (identity) instead of SymbolicValue. C# ref Box b as method receiver now works. Commit c33902d6.

title: Rust: vec![] macro lowered as SymbolicValue, not array allocation
description:
## Root Cause

`lower_macro_invocation` in `interpreter/frontends/rust/expressions.py:560` is a generic handler
that emits `CallFunction(result_reg=reg, func_name=FuncName("vec!"), args=())` for ALL macro
invocations. It extracts the macro name from the node text but **never visits the token_tree
child** — all arguments are silently dropped (args=() always).

For `vec![1, 2, 3]`, the tree-sitter AST is:
```
macro_invocation
├── identifier: "vec"
├── "!"
└── token_tree: "[1, 2, 3]"
    ├── integer_literal: "1"
    ├── integer_literal: "2"
    └── integer_literal: "3"
```

Since there is no `vec!` builtin registered in the VM, the call falls back to a SymbolicValue.

## Observed IR (wrong)
```
%26 = call_function vec!  # no args
decl_var v %26
```

## Expected IR
Should mirror lower_tuple_expr (expressions.py:643) and common_expr.lower_list_literal:
```
%26 = const 3
%27 = new_array type=list size=%26
%28 = const 1
%29 = const 0
store_index %27 %29 %28
... (repeat for elements 2 and 3)
decl_var v %27
```

## Fix

Special-case `vec!` in `lower_macro_invocation`:
1. Detect macro_name == "vec!"
2. Find the `token_tree` child node
3. Extract named children (integers, identifiers, expressions — anything `is_named`)
4. Emit NewArray(size=len(elems)) + StoreIndex per element — identical to lower_tuple_expr

The `lower_tuple_expr` pattern (expressions.py:643-668) and `common_expr.lower_list_literal`
(dispatched from array_expression at frontend.py:96) are exact precedents.

Note: `vec![x; n]` fill syntax (creates n copies of x) has a different token_tree structure
and should be xfail'd separately if not handled.

## Scope
Single function + one new helper in `interpreter/frontends/rust/expressions.py`.
One unit test (IR shape) + one integration test (v is Pointer, elements correct) — the xfail
in tests/integration/test_pointer_types.py::test_rust_vec_produces_pointer should un-xfail.
close_reason: Fixed in 23b81750: _lower_vec_macro special-cases vec! in lower_macro_invocation, emitting NewArray + StoreIndex per element. xfail test un-xfailed and strengthened with element value assertions.

title: VM: add string .length()/.size()/.Length to METHOD_TABLE
description: String method calls like .length() (Java/JS), .size() (Ruby/Kotlin), .Length (C#) return symbolic because the VM doesn't dispatch language-specific method names on raw Python strings. The METHOD_TABLE in builtins.py already handles subList/substring/slice. Add length/size/Length entries that delegate to len(). This unblocks the Java guard test (red-dragon-8h9z).
close_reason: Added length/size/Length to METHOD_TABLE delegating to len(). All languages benefit.

title: Java pattern matching: missing integration tests for type_pattern, record_pattern, guard, wildcard
description: Unit tests verify IR structure (no SYMBOLIC, correct opcodes). Missing integration tests that compile and execute Java programs using pattern matching and assert on concrete results: instanceof with binding, switch with type_pattern, guard clauses, wildcard patterns, record destructuring.
close_reason: 7 integration tests added: instanceof binding, switch type_pattern, wildcard, guard (1 xfail for symbolic guard — red-dragon-8h9z).

title: Equivalence test: Pascal factorial_iter fails after Result return fix
description: test_factorial_iter_equiv.py compares opcode sequences across all 15 languages. After red-dragon-44tw (Pascal functions return Result instead of None), Pascal's implicit return is LOAD_VAR+RETURN instead of CONST+RETURN. This is correct behavior — the equivalence test needs to accommodate Pascal's legitimate difference.
close_reason: Fixed: Pascal excluded from strict equality, divergence tested explicitly.

title: Pascal: functions return None instead of Result variable
description: Pascal functions use 'Result := expr' to set the return value. The frontend emits 'CONST None' + 'RETURN' at the end of every function body (both functions and procedures). For functions (kFunction), it should emit 'LOAD_VAR Result' + 'RETURN' instead. This is a pre-existing single-file bug, not related to multi-file support. Discovered while strengthening multi-file integration test assertions.
close_reason: Functions now emit LOAD_VAR Result + RETURN. Procedures unchanged. 507 Pascal tests pass.

title: Pascal: begin...end. program body block not lowered (blockTr node)
description: The Pascal frontend does not lower the program body 'begin...end.' block (tree-sitter node type 'blockTr'). Statements inside the main program block produce SYMBOLIC unsupported:blockTr. This prevents multi-file tests from asserting on execution results since top-level calls in the begin block are not executed. Single-file programs with only function definitions work fine.
close_reason: Fixed: BLOCK_TR dispatched to lower_pascal_block.

title: Multi-file integration tests: weak assertions for Java/Go/Rust/C/C++/C#/Kotlin/Scala/Pascal
description: test_all_languages_execution.py tests for Java, Go, Rust, C, C++, C#, Kotlin, Scala, Pascal only assert function/class name is in scope ('add' in result). They don't assert on concrete execution results. Should call the cross-module function and verify the computed value.
close_reason: Fixed: all 15 languages now assert concrete execution results.

title: ExportTable.lookup uses or-chaining — falsy values silently skipped
description: ExportTable.lookup() uses 'self.functions.get(name) or self.classes.get(name) or self.variables.get(name)'. If a label is an empty string or other falsy value, the or-chain skips it and falls through incorrectly. Should use explicit 'is not None' checks.
close_reason: Replaced or-chaining with key-in-dict iteration. Test for empty-string label added.

title: Linker import stub dropper assumes CALL_FUNCTION immediately followed by DECL_VAR
description: _transform_module uses skip_next_decl_for_reg flag + result.pop() to drop import stubs. If any instruction is inserted between CALL_FUNCTION 'import' and DECL_VAR (e.g., type annotation, SYMBOLIC), the flag remains set and could match an unrelated DECL_VAR. Should reset flag on any non-DECL_VAR instruction.
close_reason: Won't fix — theoretical. Only Python emits CALL_FUNCTION 'import', and it always emits CALL+DECL_VAR adjacently. No frontend can produce an intervening instruction. Added documentation note in _transform_module docstring referencing this issue.

title: COBOL multi-file: STOP RUN in dependency modules terminates entire program
description: The linker concatenates all modules' top-level code into one stream (deps first, entry last). COBOL's STOP RUN lowers to a RETURN/BRANCH that halts the VM. If a dependency module has STOP RUN, it terminates execution before the entry module's code runs. Real COBOL runtime semantics: CALL invokes a subprogram which returns control to the caller on STOP RUN or GOBACK. The linked model doesn't support this — it would require either stripping STOP RUN from dependency modules during linking, or implementing CALL as a true function dispatch (push frame, BRANCH to callee, GOBACK returns). For now, dependency COBOL modules must omit STOP RUN to work in multi-file mode.

title: Import extraction: Ruby/Lua/PHP bare require names incorrectly marked as system imports
description: The import extractors for Ruby, Lua, and PHP marked bare module names (require 'utils', require('mathlib')) as is_system=True because they contained no dots or slashes. This caused the resolver to skip them entirely, so local files were never discovered. Fix: set is_system=False for ambiguous bare names and let the resolver decide — if the file exists locally it's resolved, otherwise silently skipped. Commit: 70830d8.
close_reason: Fixed in 70830d8.

title: Linker: dependency modules' top-level code unreachable — variables/functions not in scope
description: Entry module IR was placed first in merged IR, so dependency modules' top-level code (STORE_VAR for variables, CONST+DECL_VAR for functions) ran after the entry module's code. Cross-module function calls and variable references returned symbolic because the bindings didn't exist yet. Also, variable import stubs (CALL_FUNCTION 'import' + DECL_VAR) overwrote concrete values set by dependency modules. Fix: place deps first in merged IR, drop import stubs for resolved names, chain module entries. Part of the same root cause as the dispatch mismatch — both fixed in the linker rewrite. Commits: 367be5c, 1fcd713.
close_reason: Fixed in 367be5c and 1fcd713. Superseded by linker rewrite (1fcd713, red-dragon-x6td).

title: Linker: VM dispatch mismatch — import stubs produce symbolic values instead of BoundFuncRef/ClassRef
description: The initial linker rewrote CALL_FUNCTION operands from local names to namespaced labels (e.g. 'add' → 'math_utils.func_add_2'), but the VM resolves function calls via local_vars scope lookup, not by label. This caused: (1) CALL_FUNCTION with namespaced label not found in scope → symbolic, (2) ClassRef.name was namespaced ('main.Circle') but class_methods keyed by bare name ('Circle') → constructor dispatch failed, (3) string func_val indexed as native string ('geometry.class_Circle_0'[5] → 't'). Fix: replace import stubs with CONST label bindings, pass merged symbol tables to ExecutionStrategies, keep bare names in symbol tables. Commits: 16f163e, 367be5c.
close_reason: Fixed in 16f163e and 367be5c. Superseded by linker rewrite (1fcd713, red-dragon-x6td).

title: Kotlin implicit-this: bare field reads/writes in methods should use symbol table
description: Not a gap — Kotlin implicit field access already works. Tested with primary constructor fields and mutable var fields in methods.

title: Scala implicit-this: bare field reads/writes in methods should use symbol table
description: Not a gap — Scala implicit field access already works. Tested with primary constructor fields and mutable var fields in methods.

title: PHP member access $c->radius returns None
description: $c = new Circle(5); $result = $c->radius; returns None. PHP member access via -> may have a lowering or execution gap.

title: Ruby class method dispatch — self.method not dispatched as static
description: class Util; def self.square(x); x * x; end; end; Util.square(5) returns symbolic. Ruby class methods (def self.method) need ClassRef dispatch.

title: Rust impl method returns () instead of field value
description: struct Circle with impl get_radius(&self) -> i32 { self.radius } — c.get_radius() returns () instead of 5. Rust impl method return value not propagated.

title: PHP static method dispatch Util::square(5) returns None
description: PHP static method calls via Class::method() return None instead of the computed value. May need ClassRef dispatch similar to Java/C#/C++.

title: C++ constructor call syntax Circle c(5) — field not stored
description: Circle c(5) constructor invocation doesn't store field values. c.radius returns None. The C++ constructor call syntax (without new) may not be dispatching through the constructor properly.

title: C struct field store/load chain broken
description: struct Circle c; c.radius = 5; int result = c.radius; — result is None. The STORE_FIELD/LOAD_FIELD chain on C structs is broken.

title: Static method dispatch — Class.method() calls return symbolic
description: Math.square(5) returns symbolic across Java, C#, C++. CALL_METHOD receives a ClassRef as the object but doesn't know how to dispatch static methods. The symbol table has the method info — executor could use it to resolve the function label and dispatch directly.

title: Implicit-this for field READS in methods — LOAD_VAR x should be LOAD_FIELD this, x
description: Deferred — needs parameter/local tracking on ctx to distinguish field reads from local variable reads. Prerequisite: track parameters in a set on ctx during function lowering, then check 'is this name a local/param? if not, and it's a field, emit LOAD_FIELD this'. Separate from cross-class fix.

title: Cross-class field resolution — subclass constructor assigning parent fields
description: class Dog extends Animal { Dog(String n) { name = n; } } — name is a field of Animal, not Dog. The symbol table only has Dog's own fields, so implicit-this detection misses inherited fields. Need to walk the class hierarchy via parents.

title: Symbol table Phase 3: replace workarounds with symbol table lookups
description: Replace _class_field_names, _resolve_match_args, _resolve_class_static_field with ctx.symbol_table lookups. Delete the ad-hoc code.

title: Symbol table Phase 2: per-language _extract_symbols implementations
description: Implement _extract_symbols for each tree-sitter frontend. Priority: C#, Java, C++, Python (have workarounds to replace), then remaining languages. Each produces a populated SymbolTable with ClassInfo/FieldInfo for all classes in the source.

title: Migrate heap object references from bare strings to Pointer objects
description: Currently NEW_OBJECT/NEW_ARRAY return bare string addresses (e.g. 'obj_0') as TypedValue with UNKNOWN type. This loses type information and creates a parallel representation alongside the Pointer dataclass. Migration: wrap all heap addresses in Pointer(base=addr, offset=0) at creation time, update _heap_addr() to unwrap Pointer.base, fix all downstream consumers (LOAD_INDEX native string indexing, _builtin_slice, ImplicitThisFieldFallback, typed_from_runtime, BINOP double-wrapping guards, test assertions). Tagged the TypedValue with the correct struct type via pointer(scalar(type_name)).

title: P1: test_function_pointer_returned_from_function name misleading
description: tests/integration/test_c_function_pointer.py:61 — test name says FP is returned from function, but the function returns an int computed via a local FP. No function pointer is actually returned. Rename to test_function_pointer_called_inside_function or similar.

title: Pascal defProc qualified method bodies not wired to class
description: Pascal defProc qualified method bodies (e.g., procedure TFoo.SetName) are lowered as standalone __anon functions instead of being wired back to the class method stub. The synthetic __set_Name__ calls SetName on the class, but SetName has an empty stub body. Need to link out-of-class procedure implementations to their class methods.

title: VM: implicit this.field resolution for bare field names in method/constructor bodies
description: When a method or constructor body references a field by bare name (e.g. 'count' instead of 'this.count'), LOAD_VAR fails to find it in local scope and produces a symbolic. The fix: when LOAD_VAR fails to find a variable in the scope chain, check if the current frame has a 'this' reference and whether the heap object has a matching field. This affects all OOP languages (Java, C#, Kotlin, Scala, C++, PHP, etc.).

title: C#: out expressions (out var) not supported

title: PHP: attributes (#[Attr]) not supported
description: NOT A BUG: PHP attributes (#[Attr]) are already silently ignored by the frontend — they don't produce SYMBOLIC unsupported. Attributes are metadata (not executable), so skipping them is correct behavior.

description: PHP 7+ anonymous classes (new class { ... }) are commonly used for one-off implementations. ANONYMOUS_CLASS node not dispatched.

title: Kotlin: property getters/setters not dispatched
description: Kotlin custom property accessors (get() = ..., set(value) { ... }) are very common. GETTER and SETTER nodes not in stmt dispatch.

title: Scala: enum definitions (Scala 3) not supported
description: Scala 3 enum definitions (enum Color { case Red, Green, Blue }) are core language syntax. ENUM_DEFINITION not dispatched.
close_reason: Implemented: simple enum definitions (enum Color { case Red, Green, Blue }). Rust-style container + STORE_FIELD. 6 tests. Parameterized variants deferred (red-dragon-y9s4).

title: Scala: extension methods (Scala 3) not supported
description: Scala 3 extension methods (extension (x: Int) def isEven = x % 2 == 0) are core language syntax. EXTENSION_DEFINITION not dispatched.

title: Scala bubble sort crashes: out-of-bounds heap array call-index falls through to string indexing
description: When CALL_FUNCTION on a heap-backed array has an out-of-bounds index, section 2b in _handle_call_function falls through to 2c, which treats the heap address string (e.g. 'arr_0') as a native string and crashes with IndexError. Root cause of bubble sort failure is actually object method scoping (methods can't write to object fields), but this crash is a separate bug that should be fixed. The 2c guard should exclude heap addresses.

title: Kotlin: secondary constructors not dispatched
description: Kotlin secondary constructors (constructor(...) { ... }) with this() delegation are common. SECONDARY_CONSTRUCTOR and CONSTRUCTOR_DELEGATION_CALL nodes not dispatched.

title: JavaScript: optional chaining (?.) not dispatched
description: Optional chaining (obj?.prop, obj?.method(), arr?.[idx]) is very common in modern JavaScript. The OPTIONAL_CHAIN node type is defined in node_types but not in the dispatch table.

title: C++: structured bindings (auto [a, b] = ...) not supported
description: C++17 structured bindings allow auto [a, b] = pair or auto [x, y, z] = tuple. STRUCTURED_BINDING_DECLARATOR is not dispatched. Common in modern C++ code.

title: TypeScript: optional chaining (?.) not dispatched
description: Optional chaining (obj?.prop, obj?.method(), arr?.[idx]) is very common in modern TypeScript. Inherits gap from JavaScript frontend.

title: Go: iota constant generator not supported
description: Go's iota is ubiquitous for defining enumerated constants (const ( A = iota; B; C )). Core language feature, not a library. Currently not handled.

title: Python: slice expressions (arr[1:3]) not handled
description: Python slice syntax like arr[1:3], arr[::2], arr[::-1] is extremely common. The SLICE node type appears to be missing from the Python frontend dispatch table. This is core Python syntax, not a library feature.
notes: Not a gap. Python slice expressions work correctly. Array slices return heap addresses (e.g. 'arr_1') which is correct VM behavior — arrays are heap-allocated. Verified: arr[1:3] on [10,20,30,40,50] produces heap object with fields {0:20, 1:30, length:2}.

title: Rust frontend: Box as real object with auto-deref insertion via frontend type tracking
description:
Box::new(x) currently uses a pass-through (returns x directly) which makes the Rosetta linked list work but breaks explicit deref (*box_expr) and type identity.

The correct approach:
1. Box::new(x) creates a real Box object with value=x (revert pass-through)
2. *box_expr → LOAD_FIELD 'value' (already works)
3. Rust frontend inserts auto-deref (LOAD_FIELD 'value') after unwrap() when the result is Box<T>

This requires frontend type tracking: the frontend must know that Option<Box<Node>>.unwrap() returns Box<Node> so it can insert the deref. This means resolving type parameters during lowering.

Current state: Box::new is pass-through, answer=6 works for Rosetta but *deref on Box produces wrong results. Box prelude class is emitted but never instantiated.

Depends on: type parameter resolution in CALL_FUNCTION operands (spec Decision 5, currently deferred)

title: P1: test_sibling_classes_are_ambiguous_with_fallback is not actually ambiguous
description: In test_overload_resolver.py: test name says 'ambiguous' but Dog matches exactly (score 2) while Cat mismatches (score -1). Resolution is unambiguous. Should be named test_sibling_class_exact_match_beats_mismatch.

title: Unify func_signatures and method_signatures into single container
description: func_signatures (flat, str-keyed) and method_signatures (class-scoped, TypeExpr-keyed) are two separate containers that get_func_signature already abstracts over. Unify into a single method_signatures dict where unbound functions go under a sentinel key (UNKNOWN). Removes the dual-container split from TypeEnvironment, _InferenceContext, and TypeEnvironmentBuilder. External API (get_func_signature) unchanged.

title: FunctionSignature: add FunctionKind enum (UNBOUND/INSTANCE/STATIC)
description: FunctionSignature has no structural way to distinguish instance methods from static methods from top-level functions. The only signal is a fragile string convention (param named 'this'). Add a FunctionKind enum and a 'kind' field to FunctionSignature, plus a callable_params property that excludes 'this' for instance methods. Frontends set the kind during lowering/inference based on class context.

title: Remove class method pollution from flat func_signatures
description: Stop copying class method types into the flat name-keyed func_return_types/func_param_types dicts. Class methods now only populate method_signatures. Flat func_signatures is reserved for standalone functions.

title: func_signatures: migrate to list[FunctionSignature] with accessor wrapper
description: Change func_signatures value type from FunctionSignature to list[FunctionSignature]. Add get_func_signature(name, index=0, class_name=ScalarType) accessor to TypeEnvironment. Migrate all direct dict access (70+ sites in tests, inference, executor) to use the accessor. Default index=0 preserves existing behavior.

title: func_signatures: class-scoped method_signatures with ScalarType keys
description: Add method_signatures: dict[ScalarType, dict[str, list[FunctionSignature]]] to TypeEnvironment, scoped by class TypeExpr. Populate from class_method_types during inference (already uses ScalarType keys). Update get_func_signature wrapper to route class-scoped lookups to method_signatures. Executor wraps type_hint string in ScalarType at the lookup boundary.

title: Type inference: label-keyed func_signatures for overload support
description: func_signatures is keyed by function name, so overloads overwrite each other. Add func_signatures_by_label (label → FunctionSignature) to TypeEnvironment for overload-safe signature lookup. Keep existing name-keyed func_signatures for backward compat. The overload resolver in .7.3.2 will use the label-keyed version.

title: C++: overload resolution integration tests
description: Integration tests: different arity overloads, same arity with type-based resolution. Zero LLM calls.

title: Java: overload resolution integration tests

title: C#: overload resolution integration tests

title: PHP field initializers use 'self' instead of '$this' in STORE_FIELD
description: PHP lower_php_property_declaration() emits STORE_FIELD [self, $count, value] using constants.PARAM_SELF ('self'). But PHP methods receive the instance as '$this' (constants.PARAM_PHP_THIS). The mismatch means field initializers write to a non-existent 'self' variable, leaving heap fields unpopulated. Fix: use LOAD_VAR '$this' instead of 'self' in the STORE_FIELD emission. See _CHAINING_SYMBOLIC_LANGUAGES in test_rosetta_method_chaining.py.
close_reason: Closed

title: C++ *this dereference lowered as LOAD_FIELD instead of no-op
description: C++ frontend lowers 'return *this' as LOAD_FIELD [this_reg, '*'], treating the unary dereference operator as a field access on a field literally named '*'. Should resolve to just 'this' since our VM references aren't real pointers. Blocks method chaining (return *this pattern). See _CHAINING_SYMBOLIC_LANGUAGES in test_rosetta_method_chaining.py.
close_reason: Closed

title: Scala implicit return from blocks not working
description: Scala functions with block bodies (def f(): Int = { expr }) do not return the last expression value. The lower_function_def handler calls ctx.lower_block(body_node) for block-type bodies, which iterates children but discards the last expression result. A standalone 'this' or 'this.count' at the end of a block should be the implicit return value. This affects method chaining (return this) and value-returning methods. Workaround: use explicit 'return' keyword. See test_rosetta_method_chaining.py where scala remains in _CHAINING_SYMBOLIC_LANGUAGES.
close_reason: Closed

title: Type narrowing in conditionals (instanceof/is/match)
description:
When the inference engine encounters instanceof (Java/TS), is (C#/Kotlin), or pattern match with type checks (Scala/Rust), it should narrow the variable's type within the true branch.

Example: if (x instanceof Animal) { x.speak(); } — inside the branch, x should be narrowed from Object to Animal. is_subtype_expr() is needed to validate that the narrowing is sound (e.g., Dog narrows to Animal because Dog <: Animal).

Requires:
1. Wire extend_with_interfaces() into infer_types() to build the augmented TypeGraph
2. Add type narrowing logic to _infer_branch_if or a new narrowing pass
3. Track narrowed types per-scope (similar to TypeScript's control flow narrowing)

This is the most likely first consumer of is_subtype_expr(). Intersects heavily with red-dragon-jn0 (pattern matching).

title: TypeGraph interface subtyping: production consumers
description:
extend_with_interfaces() adds class→interface edges to the TypeGraph so is_subtype_expr() works across interface hierarchies. Currently no production code consumes this. This epic tracks the three scenarios that would require it, each as a child story.

Prerequisite: extend_with_interfaces() itself works (tested in test_type_graph.py). The work here is wiring it into the inference/execution pipeline AND building the consumers.

Intersects with red-dragon-jn0 (pattern matching) — type narrowing from instanceof/is checks is the most likely first consumer.

title: VM: LoadRegion out-of-bounds read returns zero-padded bytes
description:
In standard COBOL, CALL USING passes raw memory by position. If the callee's LINKAGE field is declared larger than the caller's USING argument (size mismatch), the callee reads bytes beyond what the caller wrote. Real COBOL runtimes return whatever happens to be in memory; our interpreter should return zeroes for the overrun portion (safe, predictable, matches OS zero-page semantics).

Currently the LoadRegion handler in interpreter/vm/executor.py (or wherever LoadRegion is dispatched) raises an index error if offset + length exceeds the region's allocated size.

**Fix:** In the LoadRegion execution handler, clamp the actual read to min(length, region_size - offset). For any bytes beyond the region end, append zero bytes to the result. Total returned bytes = length (zero-padded to requested size).

This is a VM-level invariant: AllocRegion zero-initialises (all bytes start as 0x00). LoadRegion out-of-bounds reads return 0x00 for the overrun.

This handles the case where LINKAGE SECTION fields are wider than the USING argument passed by the caller — consistent with COBOL 'undefined but safe' semantics.
acceptance_criteria:
1. LoadRegion on a region of size N with offset=0, length=N+4 returns N real bytes followed by 4 zero bytes (no exception).
2. Unit test in tests/unit/ for the LoadRegion handler covering the padding case.
3. Existing LoadRegion tests remain green (in-bounds reads unchanged).

title: COBOL: CALL USING BY VALUE / BY CONTENT — no copy-back after CallWithMemory
description:
Once CALL USING BY REFERENCE is implemented (red-dragon-q7r1), BY VALUE and BY CONTENT need a small additional change in lower_call.py.

BY VALUE (param_type == 'VALUE') and BY CONTENT (param_type == 'CONTENT') both pass a copy to the callee. The callee cannot modify the caller's storage. The only difference from BY REFERENCE is: skip the copy-back step (step 5 in red-dragon-q7r1).

Standard COBOL semantics: BY CONTENT is identical to BY VALUE at the machine level — a copy is made, the callee operates on the copy, and no write-back occurs.

**Change in lower_call.py:** In the copy-back loop (post-CallWithMemory), only emit WriteRegion for params where param_type == 'REFERENCE'. Params with param_type in ('VALUE', 'CONTENT') are skipped.

This is a one-line conditional around the existing copy-back emission from red-dragon-q7r1.
acceptance_criteria:
1. CALL 'SUBPROG' USING BY VALUE WS-A: callee modifies its LINKAGE copy; caller's WS-A is unchanged after return.
2. Same for BY CONTENT.
3. Unit test: lower_call emits no copy-back WriteRegion for BY VALUE / BY CONTENT params.
4. Integration test: SUBPROG doubles a BY VALUE field; caller verifies WS-A is unchanged.
5. @covers(CobolFeature.CALL_USING_BY_VALUE) or equivalent feature enum member.

title: WORKING-STORAGE not persistent across subprogram calls
description:
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION is re-allocated on every call to a COBOL subprogram, wiping its state. Real COBOL semantics require WS to persist across calls (allocated once at program load, not re-initialised on re-entry).

This is a pre-existing gap not introduced by the LINKAGE/LOCAL-STORAGE implementation. It means:
- Subprograms that accumulate state in WS across multiple calls will not work correctly
- Recursive subprograms that mutate WS will lose state on re-entry

The root cause is that AllocRegion for WS is emitted at the callee's entry label, which re-executes on every call. The fix requires a separate program-load phase that allocates WS regions once, independent of the call entry path.

Related spec: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-03-cobol-linkage-local-storage-design.md (Known Limitations section)
